elements.sent-2411-001: The Schr\"odinger bridge problem (SBP) seeks to find the measure $\hat\bP$ on a certain path space which interpolates between state-space distributions $\rho_0$ at time $0$ and $\rho_T$ at time $T$ while minimizing the KL divergence (relative entropy) to a reference path measure $\bR$.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this work, we tackle the SBP in the case when $\bR$ is the path measure of a jump diffusion.
elements.sent-2411-003: Under mild assumptions, with both the operator theory approach and the stochastic calculus techniques, we establish an $h$-transform theory for jump diffusions and devise an approximation method to achieve the jump-diffusion SBP solution $\hat\bP$ as the strong-convergence limit of a sequence of harmonic $h$-transforms.
elements.sent-2411-004: To the best of our knowledge, these results are novel in the study of SBP.
elements.sent-2411-005: Moreover, the $h$-transform framework and the approximation method developed in this work are robust and applicable to a relatively general class of jump diffusions.
elements.sent-2411-006: In addition, we examine the SBP of particular types of jump diffusions under additional regularity conditions and extend the existing results on the SBP from the diffusion case to the jump-diffusion setting.
elements.sent-2411-007: In this introduction, we will cover preliminaries on the long-celebrated Schr\"odinger Bridge Problem (SBP) and the framework of jump-diffusion processes under which our work is conducted.
elements.subsection-2411-001: Schr\"odinger Bridge Problem: Formulation and Motivation
elements.sent-2411-008: The original version of this nearly century-old problem was formulated by Erwin Schr\"odinger in \cite{schrodinger1931umkehrung, schrodinger1932theorie .}
elements.sent-2411-009: In Schr\"odinger's thought experiment, a large number of hot gaseous particles are observed at time $t = 0$ to have initial distribution $\rho_0$.
elements.sent-2411-010: The observer's prior belief about the behavior of these particles is that their position should evolve according to a Markov process with transition density $q(s,x,t,y)$ for $0\leq s \leq t \leq T$.
elements.sent-2411-011: As such, by the law of large numbers, the particle density at a later time $T$ should be roughly equal to \begin{equation}\tilde{\rho}_T(\cdot) \approx \int_{\R^n} q(0, x, T, \cdot )\rho_0(x)dx.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-012: Suppose however that the actual particle density $\rho_T$ observed at time $T$ vastly differs from the expected distribution $\tilde{\rho _T$.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Schr\"odinger believed that a rare event had occurred and that one must find the ``most probable explanation'' for it.
elements.sent-2411-014: Working from intuition and without a precise statement of the problem, Schr\"odinger nevertheless correctly deduced that the solution satisfies a pair of integral equations, which would be later known as the Schr\"odinger system.
elements.sent-2411-015: More than fifty years after Schr\"odinger's work, F\"ollmer obtained in \cite{follmer1988random a rigorous formulation of the SBP using the theory of large deviations \cite{dembo1998zeitouni .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: To formulate the modern version of the SBP, we need to introduce some terminology.
elements.sent-2411-017: Let $\Omega=D([0,T];\R^n)$ be the collection of all c\`adl\`ag\footnote{A c\`adl\`ag function is right-continuous with left-limits at every point in its domain.}
elements.sent-2411-018: This is the space of trajectories, and $\omega\in \Omega$ is a sample path.
elements.sent-2411-019: Let $\Omega$ be equipped with the Skorokhod metric.
elements.sent-2411-020: The resulting Borel $\sigma$-algebra $\cB(\Omega)$ is generated by the time projections $X_t:\omega\in\Omega\mapsto\omega(t)\in \R^n$ for $t\in[0,T]$.
elements.sent-2411-021: Denote by $\cP(\Omega)$ the set of probability measures on $(\Omega,\cB(\Omega))$.
elements.sent-2411-022: We will refer to $\bR\in\cP(\Omega)$ as a path measure and $\set{X_t _{[0,T]}$ the stochastic process given by the time projections as the canonical process under $\bR$.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Let $\set{\cF_t _{[0,T]}$ be the natural filtration generated by $\set{X_t _{[0,T]}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Whenever necessary, we will adopt the usual augmentation of the natural filtration (by $\bR$ or another path measure) (Ref. \cite[II-67]{rogers2000diffusionsVOL1 ), which we continue writing as $\set{\cF_t _{[0,T]}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Let $\omega_1,...,\omega_N \in \Omega$ be independently sampled paths according to a ``reference'' path measure $\bR\in\cP(\Omega)$, and denote their empirical distribution by $Z_N$.
elements.sent-2411-026: Then as $N\to\infty$, Sanov's theorem \cite{sanov1961probability gives the (asymptotic) likelihood that the empirical distribution of these paths deviates from its true distribution $\bR$: \begin{equation}\text{Prob}(Z_N\in A) \approx \exp(-N\inf_{\bP\in A} \KL{\bP}{\bR}) \forall A \subseteq \cP(\Omega),\end{equation} where $\KL{\cdot {\cdot}$ is the Kullback-Leibler divergence (KL divergence), also known as the relative entropy.}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Therefore, the modern formulation of the SBP aims to bridge the two endpoint distributions $\rho_0,\rho_T$ while staying as close as possible (in the sense of KL divergence) to the reference path measure $\bR$.
elements.sent-2411-029: For a path measure $\bP\in\cP(\Omega)$, let $\bP_t\in \cP(\R^n)$ be its marginal distribution at time $t$, meaning that for any Borel $B\subseteq\R^n$, $\bP_t(B)=\bP(\set{X_t\in B )$.}
elements.sent-2411-030: The modern formulation of the SBP states: \begin{dfn [Schr\"odinger Bridge Problem] Given a reference path measure $\bR\in \cP(\Omega)$ and target endpoint distributions $\rho_0,\rho_T \in \cP(\R^n)$, determine \begin{equation}\label{Definition:DynamicSBP} \hat{\bP}=\arg\min_{\bP \in \cP(\Omega) }\set{\KL{\bP}{\bR} \text{ such that } \bP_0=\rho_0,\bP_T=\rho_T }.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Early work on the SBP considered the reference measure $\bR$ being the law of a Brownian motion (Wiener process), and it was later generalized to the case when the canonical process under $\bR$ is a general $\R^n$-valued diffusion process solving the It\^o Stochastic Differential Equation (SDE) \begin{equation}dX_t=b(t,X_t)dt+\sigma(t,X_t)dB_t,\end{equation} where $\set{B_t _{t\geq0}$ is a standard $m$-dimensional Brownian motion.}
elements.sent-2411-032: In the present work, we extend this framework to consider diffusion processes with jumps.
elements.sent-2411-034: We consider $\bR\in\cP(\Omega)$ under which the canonical process $\set{X_t _{t\geq0}$ satisfies the following L\'evy-It\^o SDE: \begin{equation}\label{ReferenceLevyIto} \begin{aligned} dX_t=b(t,X_{t-}) dt+\sigma(t,X_{t-})dB_t + \int_{|z|\leq 1}\gamma(t,X_{t-} ,z)\tilde{N}(dt,dz) + \int_{|z|>1}\gamma(t,X_{t-} ,z)N(dt,dz), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\set{B_t _{t\geq 0}$ is a standard $m$-dimensional Brownian motion, $\{N(dt,dz)\}_{t\geq 0}$ is an $\ell$-dimensional jump measure associated with a Poisson process with intensity $\nu(dz)$, $\tilde{N (dt,dz)$ is the compensated jump measure, i.e., $\tilde{N (dt,dz):= N(dt,dz)-\nu(dz)dt$, and $\set{B_t _{t\geq 0}$ and $\set{N(dt,dz) _{t\geq 0}$ are independent.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: The coefficients $\sigma$, $b$ and $\gamma$ are required to satisfy some mild conditions, which will be stated later, to guarantee that all the stochastic integrals in \eqref{ReferenceLevyIto are well-defined.}
elements.sent-2411-036: A process $\set{X_t _{t\geq0}$ solving the SDE (\ref{ReferenceLevyIto ) is called a jump diffusion, also called jump-diffusion process or diffusion process with jumps in the literature.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: We will use the name diffusion process or simply diffusion to refer to the case $\gamma=0$.
elements.sent-2411-038: The additional integral terms in (\ref{ReferenceLevyIto ) introduce a jump component with a general jump coefficient $\gamma$.}
elements.sent-2411-039: As a result, our framework encompasses a large class of processes with drift, diffusion and jump dynamics.

elements.sent-2411-053: Schr\"odinger's original motivation for posing his problem was based in quantum physics.
elements.sent-2411-054: Over the years, as the theory behind Schr\"odinger bridges developed, they have seen applications in various fields by providing a framework for modeling the evolution of a dynamical system over time.
elements.sent-2411-055: The discovery of the connection of the SBP to the problem of Optimal Transport (OT), as well as the stochastic control formulation of the SBP, prompted a new wave of interest.
elements.sent-2411-056: While the field of OT is fundamentally concerned with finding the mapping between distributions $\rho_0$, $\rho_T$ that minimizes a cost function, the SBP inherently provides a way to reconstruct and to model the dynamics of the process as it evolves from $\rho_0$ to $\rho_T$.
elements.sent-2411-057: In particular, the static SBP discussed in the next subsection is equivalent to the entropy-regularized OT problem.
elements.sent-2411-058: In view of this, the dynamic Schr\"odinger bridge is often seen as an ``entropic interpolation'' of optimal transport.
elements.sent-2411-059: As for the stochastic control formulation of the SBP, in the setting of diffusion processes, it converges to the Benamou-Brenier (fluid dynamic) formulation of OT as the diffusion coefficient tends to $0$.
elements.sent-2411-060: These rich connections have been extensively studied \cite{mikami1990variational,dai1991stochastic,mikami2004monge,mikami2006duality,mikami2008optimal,mikami2008optimal2,leonard2013survey, chen2021optimal, chen2021stochastic .}
elements.sent-2411-061: During the last decade, these topics have been increasingly studied for their applications in machine learning \cite{wang2021deep, de2021diffusion(MachineLearning),liu2022deep,diefenbacher2024improving,peluchetti2023diffusion,tzen2019neural,tzen2019theoretical,stromme2023sampling,pavon2021data,shi2024diffusion,somnath2023aligned,vargas2021solving , where diffusion Schr\"odinger Bridges and diffusion models in general are steadily rising in popularity.}
elements.sent-2411-062: To cite some examples, Schr\"odinger Bridges have been adopted for generative modeling tasks \cite{de2021diffusion(MachineLearning) , their theoretical properties in neural networks have been investigated in \cite{tzen2019theoretical , and numerical approximations of SBP solutions have been developed in \cite{vargas2021solving .}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: While having a vast range of applications, diffusion models also have limitations as they only describe dynamics that are continuous in time.
elements.sent-2411-064: However, in the study of certain phenomena, it is beneficial or even necessary to adopt models that accommodate ``jumps'', for which L\'evy processes are natural candidates.
elements.sent-2411-065: For example, due to their nature of combining continuous and jump dynamics, L\'evy processes have been widely adopted in applications to finance as more realistic financial models \cite{tankov2003financial,eberlein2001application,schoutens2003levy,jeanblanc2009mathematical,carr2002fine .}
elements.sent-2411-066: More recently, jump diffusions\footnote{In finance literature, the word jump diffusion often refers to the specific case where the jump component is a compound Poisson process.}
elements.sent-2411-067: As an extension of both classical diffusions and L\'evy processes, jump diffusions are promising tools in creating richer models in various applied fields, such as physics, economics, and machine learning.
elements.sent-2411-068: Lying in the intersection of operator theory and stochastic analysis, the comparison between diffusions and jump diffusions has lead to many interesting problems.
elements.sent-2411-069: While the SBP for diffusions is considerably well understood, theoretical results on the counterpart problem for jump diffusions are still scarce.
elements.sent-2411-070: Building a comprehensive theory for the jump-diffusion SBP will not only expand the existing literature, but also shed light on potential new areas of applications.
elements.sent-2411-071: For these reasons, we set out to conduct a thorough study of the SBP for jump diffusions in this paper.

elements.sent-2411-222: To understand the dynamics of the jump-diffusion Schr\"odinger bridge, one must investigate properties of the $h$-transform in the jump-diffusion setting.
elements.sent-2411-223: We will first review preliminary notions on $h$-transforms, and then study the $h$-transform of a jump diffusion through (1) the generator of the jump diffusion and (2) the SDE corresponding to the jump diffusion.
elements.sent-2411-224: Following each approach, we develop a theory of $h$-transforms that extends the existing literature from the diffusion case ($\gamma = 0$) to the jump-diffusion setting.
elements.sent-2411-225: The ``jumping behavior'' of our model allows us to study a broader class of dynamics, but it also introduces an additional layer of complexity to the problem.
elements.sent-2411-226: In the generator approach, the jump component turns a local operator into a non-local one\footnote{Sometimes, the jump-diffusion operator considered in this work is also referred to as a local-nonlocal mixed-type operator in the literature.}

elements.section-2411-004: SBP for Jump Diffusions: the General Theory
elements.sent-2411-330: In this section, we return to the Schr\"odinger bridge problem (SBP) for jump diffusions and study its solution under the theory of $h$-transforms developed in Section \ref{Section:hTransform .}
elements.sent-2411-331: Recall from Theorem \ref{Theorem:fg-existence that, given a Markov reference measure $\bR$ and target endpoint distributions $\rho_0,\rho_T$, if $(\f,\g)$ is the solution to the Schr\"odinger system \eqref{Equation:fgSchrodingerSystem , then the solution to the SBP \eqref{Definition:DynamicSBP is given by $\hat{\bP =\f(X_0)\g(X_T)\bR$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-332: We aim at connecting $\hat{\bP}$ with a certain $h$-transform of the underlying path measure.
elements.sent-2411-333: In the following subsections, we first present the solution to the SBP for a pure jump process, which is a concrete example that falls outside the usual literature on the SBP for diffusions.
elements.sent-2411-334: More generally, whenever $\rho_0$ is a point mass, we can in fact determine explicitly $(\f,\g)$ and $\hat\bP$.
elements.sent-2411-335: However, for general $\rho_0,\rho_T$, it is difficult, if not impossible, to derive explicit expressions for $(\f,\g)$.
elements.sent-2411-336: Instead, we hope to approach $\hat{\bP}$ from an $h$-transform point of view by considering the function $\h(t,x):=\int \g(y)P_{t,T (x,dy)$.}
elements.sent-2411-337: Although in general we do not have any regularity result on $\g$ a priori to guarantee that $\h(t,x)$ is harmonic, we can establish an approximation theory under the assumption \ref{Assumption:Hypo .}
elements.sent-2411-338: In particular, we will prove that the desired SBP solution $\hat{\bP}$ is achieved as the limit, under strong convergence, of a family of path measures all of which are $h$-transforms by harmonic functions.

elements.section-2411-005: SBP for Jump Diffusions: when Density Exists
elements.sent-2411-442: So far we have been studying the SBP for general jump diffusions under Assumptions \ref{Assumption:MartingaleProblem-s,x , \ref{Assumption:SDEsolution and \ref{Assumption:Hypo .}}}
elements.sent-2411-443: These assumptions, especially \ref{Assumption:Hypo , are better understood for jump diffusions that admit densities.}
elements.sent-2411-444: A canonical scenario in which a density function may exist is when the jump diffusion has sufficiently regular coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-445: In particular, models with smooth coefficients have been well studied in the context of stochastic flows \cite{ishikawa2006malliavin,cass2009smooth,kunita2011analysis,kunita2013nondegenerate,kunita2019stochastic .}
elements.sent-2411-446: In this section, we first adopt the setting of Kunita \cite{kunita2019stochastic to study the SBP for jump-diffusion stochastic flows, where, under the presence of smooth\footnote{To be precise, the density functions are $C^1$ in temporal variables and $C^\infty$ in spatial variables.}}
elements.sent-2411-447: By doing so, we generalize in various degrees the known results about the diffusion SBP to the jump-diffusion setting.
elements.sent-2411-448: In addition, based on an approximation method, we extend our study to the SBP for jump diffusions with stable-like jump components.
elements.sent-2411-449: For this type of models, the density is known to exist in many situations, including when the coefficients are less regular \cite{czq2016heatkernel,chen2016heat,chen2020heat,jin2017heat .}
elements.subsection-2411-012: Schr\"odinger System for Jump Diffusions\label{subsection:varphi_hat varphi via f_g}
elements.sent-2411-450: Recall that the pair $(\varphi,\hat\varphi)$ in the diffusion case is introduced via the Fortet-Beurling-Jamison existence theorem (Theorem \ref{Theorem:FBJ-Existence ), which requires the canonical process under the reference measure to admit a strictly positive and continuous transition density function.}
elements.sent-2411-451: In addition, to express the dynamics of the diffusion SBP solution, additional regularity conditions on the coefficients, as well as assumptions on the integrability of $\g$, are imposed to ensure that the function $\varphi(t,x)$ defined in \eqref{Definition:phiphihat-introduction is harmonic, i.e., $\varphi$ is of class $C^{1,2}$ and satisfies $\cL\varphi=0$ \cite{jamison1975markov,dai1991stochastic .}}
elements.sent-2411-452: The existence of such a transition density and the corresponding harmonic function is well understood in the diffusion case, but the analogous result for jump-diffusion processes in general is not readily available.
elements.sent-2411-453: Nonetheless, in this section we will investigate certain jump diffusions whose regularity theory is better understood and we will give a careful study of the SBP associated with these jump diffusions.
elements.sent-2411-454: We begin with the assumption that the conclusion of Theorem \ref{Theorem:fg-existence holds and $(\f,\g)$ is a pair of non-negative, measurable functions that solve the Schr\"odinger system (\ref{Equation:fgSchrodingerSystem ).}}
elements.sent-2411-455: As in the diffusion case, we work under the setting where the reference endpoint distributions $\bR_0,\bR_T$ and the target ones $\rho_0,\rho_T$ all admit densities with respect to the Lebesgue measure, which we will write as $\bR_0(x)dx$, etc, and the transition density function exists everywhere and at all time, i.e., for every $0\leq s\leq t\leq T$ and $x\in \R^n$, $P_{s,t (x,dy)=p(s,x,t,y)dy$.}

elements.sent-2411-596: To begin, we cover some preliminary notions required to add a jump component to a diffusion process.
elements.sent-2411-597: We will introduce the Poisson random measure and the Poisson stochastic integral, which will then allow us to define the notion of L\'evy-It\^o process.
elements.sent-2411-598: We will conclude with It\^o's formula and the Girsanov theorem for L\'evy-It\^o processes.
elements.sent-2411-599: These notions are covered in standard jump-diffusion textbooks such as \cite{applebaum_2009,oksendal:hal-02411121,kunita2019stochastic .}
elements.sent-2411-600: The goal of this subsection is to give a mathematical interpretation of the jump component considered in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-601: It is a pair of stochastic integrals of the form \begin{equation}\label{Definition:JumpIntegrals} \int_0^t\int_{|z|\leq 1} \gamma(s,\omega,z)\tilde{N}(ds,dz)(\omega)+\int_0^t\int_{|z|>1} \gamma(s,\omega,z)N(ds,dz)(\omega).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-602: Here, $N$ is a Poisson random measure and $\tilde{N}$ is a compensated Poisson random measure, which we will define rigorously later in the section.
elements.sent-2411-603: We require some preparatory steps to define the measures $N,\tilde{N}$ and the integrals \eqref{Definition:JumpIntegrals .}
elements.sent-2411-604: Let $(\Omega,\cF,\set{\cF_t _{t\geq 0 ,\P)$ be a filtered probability space, where the filtration satisfies the ``usual assumptions'' (completeness and right-continuity).}}
elements.sent-2411-606: Some simple examples of L\'evy processes include the Wiener process (Brownian Motion) and the Poisson process.
elements.sent-2411-607: A L\'evy process always has a c\`adl\`ag (right-continuous, left-limit) modification, so we assume without loss of generality that $\set{\eta(t) _{t\geq 0}$ is c\`adl\`ag.}
elements.sent-2411-608: It is known that every L\'evy process can be decomposed into the sum of a Brownian motion (up to rotation and rescaling) with a drift and an independent jump process, and the latter is entirely determined by an intensity measure $\nu$ as follows.
elements.sent-2411-609: Let $\Delta\eta(t):=\eta(t)-\eta(t-)$ denote the jump of the L\'evy process at time $t$.
elements.sent-2411-610: Define the Poisson random measure $N(t,\cdot)$ on sets $U\in\cB(\R^\ell\setminus\set{0 )$ (Borel sets whose closure does not contain 0) by: \begin{equation}N(t,U)=N(t,U)(\omega):=\sum_{0<s\leq t}\one_U(\Delta\eta(s))(\omega).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-611: This measure counts the number of jumps of size $\Delta\eta(s)\in U$ up to time $t$.
elements.sent-2411-612: We will write its differential form as $N(dt,dz)$; this can be thought of as the number of jumps of size $\Delta\eta\in[z,z+dz]$ in the time interval $[t,t+dt]$.
elements.sent-2411-613: The L\'evy measure of the process, also called the jump intensity, is the expected number of jumps up to time $t=1$, meaning \begin{equation}\nu(U):=\bE_{\P}[N(1,U)].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-614: In particular, for fixed $U$, $\set{N(t,U) _{t\geq 0}$ is a Poisson process of intensity $\lambda=\nu(U)$.}

elements.sent-2411-627: By adding the stochastic integrals \eqref{Definition:JumpIntegrals to a classic It\^o process, one obtains L\'evy-It\^o process, also known as It\^o-L\'evy process in \cite{oksendal:hal-02411121 or L\'evy-Type Stochastic Integral in \cite{applebaum_2009 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-628: An $\R^n$-valued L\'evy-It\^o process $\set{X_t _{[0,T]}$ is a stochastic integral of the form, for every $\omega\in\Omega$, \begin{equation}\label{Equation:1DLevyItoProcess} \begin{aligned} X_t(\omega)=X_0(\omega) +\int_0^tb(s,\omega)ds+\int_0^t\sigma(s,\omega)dB_s(\omega) + \int_0^t\int_{|z|\leq 1} \gamma(s,\omega,z)\tilde{N}(ds,dz)(\omega) + \int_0^t\int_{|z|>1} \gamma(s,\omega,z)N(ds,dz)(\omega) \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $b:[0,T]\times\Omega \to \R^n$, $\sigma:[0,T]\times\Omega \to \R^n\times\R^m$, $\gamma:[0,T]\times \Omega\times\R^\ell \to \R^n$, and the integrands are predictable and satisfy the growth condition \begin{equation}\label{LevyItoGrowthCondition} \int_0^T\pran{|b(s,\omega)|+\sum_{i,j}\sigma_{i,j}^2(s,\omega)+\int_{|z|\leq 1}|\gamma(s,\omega,z)|^2\nu(dz)}ds<\infty \text{for a.e. $\omega$}.\end{equation}. \end{dfn.}}
elements.sent-2411-629: Let us now introduce our main tools for studying L\'evy-It\^o processes.
elements.sent-2411-630: The first major tool of L\'evy-It\^o calculus that we employ in this work is a generalization of It\^o's formula.

elements.subsection-2411-018: Connections between SBP and Other Fields
elements.sent-2411-716: Over the years, as the theory behind Schr\"odinger bridges developed, it has seen applications in various fields.
elements.sent-2411-717: The discovery of the connection between SBP and Optimal Transport (OT), as well as the stochastic control formulation of SBP, prompted a new wave of interest.
elements.sent-2411-718: While the field of OT is fundamentally concerned with finding the mapping between distributions $\rho_0$, $\rho_T$ that minimizes a cost function, the SBP inherently provides a way to reconstruct the dynamics of the process as it evolves from $\rho_0$ to $\rho_T$.
elements.sent-2411-719: These rich connections have been extensively studied \cite{dai1991stochastic, mikami2004monge, leonard2013survey, chen2021optimal .}
elements.sent-2411-720: In the last decade, these topics have attracted intense attention due to their applications in machine learning \cite{wang2021deep, de2021diffusion(MachineLearning),liu2022deep,tzen2019theoretical,pavon2021data,shi2024diffusion,vargas2021solving , where diffusion SBP models are steadily rising in popularity.}
elements.sent-2411-721: To cite some examples, diffusion Schr\"odinger bridges have been adopted for generative modeling tasks \cite{de2021diffusion(MachineLearning) , their theoretical properties in neural networks have been investigated in \cite{tzen2019theoretical , and their numerical approximations have been developed in \cite{vargas2021solving .}}}
